about.title: About Lzecher
about.subtitle: A free gift to Klal Yisrael — honoring memory through the timeless power of Torah learning.
about.mission: Lzecher was created as a free initiative, inspired by the timeless Jewish tradition of learning Torah for the elevation of departed souls. It is a gift to Klal Yisrael — never charging fees, never showing ads. We believe that when a community comes together to learn Torah l'iluy nishmas a loved one, the collective merit is immeasurable.
about.vision: We believe that technology can serve the highest purposes. By providing a modern, intuitive platform, we remove the logistical barriers that often make it difficult to coordinate communal learning. No more spreadsheets, no more phone calls — just a clean, simple experience that lets people focus on what matters most: the learning itself.
about.howItHelps: Lzecher supports four learning tracks — Mishnayos, Tehillim, Shnayim Mikra, and Mitzvot — in four languages: Hebrew, English, Spanish, and French. A family member can set up a memorial page in minutes, choose their tracks, and share a link with family, friends, and community members around the world. Participants claim their portions and mark them complete, with everyone able to see the collective progress in real time.
about.community: Lzecher is built for Klal Yisrael. It is completely free to use and open to all who wish to participate in this beautiful mitzvah. We are committed to maintaining the highest standards of respect, accuracy, and halachic integrity in everything we do. No fees, no ads, no compromises — just Torah learning as a zechus for the neshamot of our loved ones.
about.dedication: The Torah learned through this platform is dedicated l'iluy nishmas our beloved ones. Their families find comfort in the unity and devotion of those who learn on their behalf.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.intro: Lzecher ("we", "our", or "us") respects your privacy. This Privacy Policy explains how we collect, use, and protect your personal information when you use our platform at lzecher.com.
privacy.collection_content: We collect the following types of information: your email address when you create an account (required for authentication); your name if you choose to provide it when claiming a portion (optional); memorial project content you create, including names, biographies, dates, and family messages; photos you upload for memorial pages; and basic usage data to improve the platform experience.
privacy.use_content: We use your information solely for the stated purposes of the Lzecher platform: to operate and improve the service, display memorial pages and track learning progress, send notifications about portions you have claimed or projects you are involved in, and communicate important updates about the service. We will never use your data for advertising, profiling, or any purpose unrelated to the platform.
privacy.sharing_content: We do not sell, rent, or trade your personal information. Your name may be displayed on memorial pages when you claim or complete a portion. We share data only with the infrastructure providers necessary to operate the platform: Firebase (Google Cloud) for authentication and database, Vercel for hosting, and Resend for transactional email delivery. These providers process data solely on our behalf and in accordance with their own privacy policies.
privacy.retention_content: Memorial pages are permanent by design — they serve as lasting tributes and are intended to remain accessible indefinitely. If a project creator wishes to delete a memorial page, they may do so from their dashboard, which will permanently remove the memorial and all associated data. Account data is retained as long as your account is active.
privacy.security_content: We take reasonable measures to protect your personal information from unauthorized access, alteration, or destruction. All data is transmitted over HTTPS and stored in secured cloud infrastructure. However, no method of transmission over the internet is completely secure.
privacy.rights_content: You have the right to access the personal information we hold about you, request correction of any inaccurate data, request deletion of your account and associated data, and request an export of your data in a portable format. To exercise any of these rights, please contact us at support@lzecher.com.
privacy.contact_content: If you have questions about this Privacy Policy, please contact us at support@lzecher.com.

terms.title: Terms of Service
terms.intro: Welcome to Lzecher. By using our platform, you agree to these Terms of Service. Please read them carefully.
terms.eligibility_content: You must be at least 13 years of age to use Lzecher. By creating an account, you confirm that you meet this requirement.
terms.accounts_content: You are responsible for maintaining the security of your account and for all activity that occurs under your account. You must provide accurate information when creating a memorial page.
terms.content_content: You retain ownership of the content you submit, including biographies and memorial details. By posting content on Lzecher, you grant us a license to display it on the platform. All content must be respectful, accurate, and appropriate. We reserve the right to remove content that violates these standards.
terms.conduct_content: You agree not to: misuse the platform or use it for any purpose other than organizing Torah learning; submit false, misleading, or inappropriate content; impersonate another person or misrepresent your identity; attempt to disrupt, interfere with, or compromise the security of the service; use automated tools to scrape or collect data from the platform; or upload content that is offensive, hateful, or disrespectful to the memory of the departed.
terms.moderation_content: All memorial pages are subject to review before publication. Our moderation process ensures that content is respectful, accurate, and appropriate. We reserve the right to reject, edit, or remove any content at our sole discretion, including but not limited to content that is inappropriate, offensive, misleading, or violates these Terms. Users whose content is rejected will be notified with a reason for the decision.
terms.termination_content: We may suspend or terminate your access to the platform at any time if you violate these terms or engage in behavior that is harmful to other users or the service.
terms.liability_content: Lzecher is provided "as is" and "as available" without warranties of any kind, either express or implied. We do not guarantee that the service will be uninterrupted, error-free, or secure. We are not liable for any direct, indirect, incidental, or consequential damages arising from your use of the platform. Lzecher is a platform for organizing Torah learning and is not a halachic authority.
terms.governing_content: These Terms of Service shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, United States of America, without regard to its conflict of law provisions. Any disputes arising from these terms shall be resolved in the courts of New York.
terms.changes_content: We may update these Terms of Service from time to time. We will notify users of significant changes via email or a notice on the platform. Continued use of the platform after changes are posted constitutes acceptance of the revised terms.
terms.contact_content: If you have questions about these Terms of Service, please contact us at support@lzecher.com.

halachicGuidance.title: Halachic Guidance
halachicGuidance.subtitle: Torah sources and perspectives on learning as a merit for the departed.
halachicGuidance.intro: The concept of learning Torah l'iluy nishmas (for the elevation of the soul) is deeply rooted in Jewish tradition and supported by numerous halachic sources. The learning of Torah, recitation of Tehillim, and performance of mitzvot dedicated to the memory of the departed are among the most meaningful ways we can benefit their neshamot. Below is an overview of the halachic foundations for each learning track offered on Lzecher.
halachicGuidance.mishnayos_content: The widespread custom of learning Mishnayos for the niftar is based on the well-known teaching from the Zohar that the letters of "Mishna" (משנה) are the same as "Neshama" (נשמה), indicating a deep spiritual connection between Mishna study and the soul. The Shulchan Aruch (Yoreh De'ah 376) discusses the practice of learning Torah for the departed, and the Mishna Berura and various Acharonim emphasize the special power of Mishna learning in particular. It is customary to complete all of Shisha Sidrei Mishna within the first year after passing, and again on each yahrzeit. When divided among many participants, the collective completion carries an even greater zechus.
halachicGuidance.tehillim_content: Reciting Tehillim (Psalms) has been a universal Jewish practice for centuries, both as a merit for the living and for the departed. King David composed the Psalms with Ruach HaKodesh (divine inspiration), and their recitation carries a special spiritual power that transcends all other forms of prayer. The Chida (Rabbi Chaim Yosef David Azulai) discusses at length the profound merit that Tehillim generates for the departed, and it is a practice embraced by all communities — Ashkenazi, Sephardi, and Mizrachi alike. Completing the entire Sefer Tehillim as a community multiplies this merit.
halachicGuidance.shnayimMikra_content: The obligation to read the weekly Torah portion twice in the original text and once in Aramaic translation (or with Rashi's commentary) is rooted in the Gemara (Berachos 8a), which teaches that a person should always complete their parshiyos with the community. This is a well-established halachic obligation that Jews fulfill every week. Dedicating this weekly learning as a merit for a loved one adds a beautiful dimension of chessed shel emes — true kindness performed for those who cannot repay it. By organizing the 54 parshiyos among participants, Lzecher enables a full year of Shnayim Mikra dedication.
halachicGuidance.mitzvot_content: The Gemara in Kiddushin (40b) teaches that studying Torah about a mitzvah is considered as if one has performed it. When this study is dedicated l'iluy nishmas, it carries profound spiritual significance, generating merit for the neshama as though the departed themselves had engaged in the learning. Studying the 613 mitzvot provides a comprehensive connection to the entirety of the Torah's commandments, and when divided among many participants, it creates a powerful collective merit.
halachicGuidance.kabalos_content: Personal kabalos are commitments individuals take upon themselves as a zechus for the neshama of the departed. Unlike Torah learning which is divided into discrete units, kabalos are personal commitments that any number of participants can take on simultaneously — the more who commit, the greater the zechus for the neshama. Examples include shemiras halashon, daily tzedakah, hakaras hatov to Hashem, lighting Shabbos candles, hafrashas challah, bikur cholim, or any personal kabalah. The Gemara in Kiddushin (40b) teaches that the acceptance of a mitzvah commitment is itself considered as if one has performed it, creating immediate spiritual merit.
halachicGuidance.general_content: The merit of communal Torah learning is considered especially powerful. When multiple people join together to learn, the collective zechus is greater than the sum of its parts. The Midrash teaches that Torah learned in a group carries a unique dimension of kedusha. Lzecher facilitates this by making it simple to divide and coordinate learning among many participants, enabling families and communities to unite in this beautiful act of chessed shel emes.
halachicGuidance.disclaimer: While Lzecher is built with halachic principles in mind, it is not a halachic authority. For specific questions about your family's customs or commitments, please consult your Rav. We are working on obtaining haskamot from leading Rabbanim and will publish them here as they arrive.
